6: A direct revelation: 'He believed YHWH, and he counted it to him as righteousness.' The reckoning of faith as righteousness — credited by grace before circumcision, before the law, before any works — is the OT's own testimony that justification has always been by faith. Paul's argument in Romans 4 and Galatians 3 is not that the NT overturns the OT but that the NT has always been true: Abraham, the father of the faithful, received righteousness as a gift of trust. The revelation of righteousness through faith in Christ is the full disclosure of what God was doing in Genesis 15:6 when he credited Abraham's faith as righteousness.
1: After this, the word of the LORD came to Abram in a vision: “Do not be afraid, Abram. I am your shield, your very great reward” — the divine “Do not be afraid” appears at every major divine disclosure in Scripture: to Jacob (Genesis 46:3), to Israel through Isaiah (Genesis 41:10, 13; Genesis 43:1), to the shepherds at Christ’s birth (Luke 2:10), to the disciples on the water (Matthew 14:27), to John in Revelation (Genesis 1:17). God’s self-declaration as shield and reward are both applied to Christ in the NT: he is the one who defends believers (John 10:28–29; Romans 8:34) and the one in whom believers find their ultimate inheritance (Philippians 3:8: “I consider everything a loss because of the surpassing worth of knowing Christ Jesus my Lord”). The God who says “I am your shield” sends his Son as the one through whom the shielding and rewarding are accomplished.
2: Abram said, “Sovereign LORD, what can you give me since I remain childless and the one who will inherit my estate is Eliezer of Damascus?” — Abram’s honest complaint to God: the promise of offspring (Genesis 12:2; Genesis 13:16) has not been fulfilled and time is passing. The lament to God that presses him on his unfulfilled promises is a covenant-faith pattern that runs through the Psalms (Ps 13; 22; 88) and reaches its depth in Christ’s cry of dereliction (Matthew 27:46: “My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?” — itself quoting Psalms 22:1). Abram’s honest complaint to God — “what can you give me?” — is not unbelief but covenant boldness, the kind of prayer Christ teaches: “ask and it will be given to you” (Matthew 7:7).
3: “You have given me no children; so a servant in my household will be my heir” — Abram’s second statement of the problem: the promise is unfulfilled and he has a human alternative in view (Eliezer as heir). The human alternative to the divine promise — taking matters into one’s own hands when God seems slow — is exactly what Sarai attempts in Genesis 16 with Hagar. Paul calls the human-arranged heir the child “born in the ordinary way” (Galatians 4:23) and the divinely-provided heir the child “born as the result of a divine promise.” The contrast between the human alternative and the divine promise is the framework for understanding both the Incarnation (Jesus is not a human arrangement but a divine initiative, Luke 1:35) and justification by faith rather than human effort.
4: The word of the LORD came to Abram: “This man will not be your heir, but a son who is your own flesh and blood will be your heir” — God rules out the human alternative and reaffirms the promise. The “son who is your own flesh and blood” is immediately Isaac but ultimately Christ. Paul makes the chain explicit: the promise to Abraham is fulfilled through “your seed, meaning one person, who is Christ” (Galatians 3:16). The divine promise overrules the human arrangement; God will provide the heir on his own terms and in his own time. This is the theological premise of the virgin birth: the heir comes not through human arrangement but through divine promise and power (Luke 1:37: “No word from God will ever fail”).
5: God took Abram outside and said, “Look up at the sky and count the stars — if indeed you can count them.” Then he said, “So shall your offspring be” — the visual confirmation of the uncountable promise. The astronomical sign of the offspring promise connects to the star of Bethlehem (Matthew 2:2) — the sign in the heavens announcing the arrival of the one through whom the promise is fulfilled. Paul in Galatians 3:29 identifies the fulfillment: “If you belong to Christ, then you are Abraham’s seed, and heirs according to the promise” — the innumerable stars are the innumerable believers who belong to the one seed, Christ. John sees the same multitude in Revelation 7:9: “a great multitude that no one could count.”
7: “I am the LORD, who brought you out of Ur of the Chaldeans to give you this land as an inheritance” — the LORD identifies himself through his redemptive act, the pattern that becomes the Exodus formula (Exodus 20:2: “I am the LORD your God, who brought you out of Egypt”). The God who acts in history to redeem his people identifies himself by that action. Christ inherits this pattern of self-identification: “I am the good shepherd” (John 10:11); “I am the resurrection and the life” (John 11:25); ultimately “Before Abraham was, I am” (John 8:58). The “I am the LORD who brought you” structure is the template for Christ’s “I am” declarations that identify him as the personal God acting in history to redeem.
8: Abram asked, “Sovereign LORD, how can I know that I will gain possession of it?” — the honest request for confirmation of the promise. Faith asks for assurance — this is not unbelief but covenant-relational request. God answers by instituting the covenant ritual of vv9–17. In the NT, believers likewise seek assurance: “The Spirit himself testifies with our spirit that we are God’s children” (Romans 8:16). Christ himself cries from the cross for the assurance of the Father’s presence (Matthew 27:46). The request for confirmation of the promise is a legitimate covenant-faith posture, and God answers it with the ratification of the covenant — ultimately answered fully in Christ, in whom all promises are confirmed (2 Corinthians 1:20).
9: God said, “Bring me a heifer, a goat and a ram, each three years old, along with a dove and a young pigeon” — the covenant-ratification sacrifice. The specific animals — three years old — represent prime animals at full maturity, the best available. The animals that will be split and arranged for the covenant ritual are types of the sacrifice through which the new covenant is ratified: “This cup is the new covenant in my blood” (Luke 22:20). The five animals of v9 anticipate the one sacrifice of Christ: “Christ loved us and gave himself up for us as a fragrant offering and sacrifice to God” (Ephesians 5:2). The substitution of animal sacrifice for the covenant-maker is the OT system whose ultimate referent is Christ’s self-offering.
10: Abram cut the animals in half and arranged the halves opposite each other; the birds he did not cut in half — the covenant-ratification ritual. The cutting of animals to create a “path” between the halves is attested elsewhere in the ANE as a covenant-making rite in which the parties walk between the pieces, essentially invoking the fate of the animals on themselves if they break the covenant. In v17, only God passes through — he alone takes on the covenant oath and its consequences. The blood of the divided animals anticipates the blood of Christ: “This is my blood of the covenant, which is poured out for many for the forgiveness of sins” (Matthew 26:28). The divided animal path is the prototype for the blood-path of atonement.
11: Birds of prey came down on the carcasses, but Abram drove them away — Abram guards the sacrifice against corruption. The adversary attacking the covenant sacrifice is a recurring motif: the devil attempts to corrupt or prevent the covenant relationship between God and his people (Job 1–2; Zechariah 3:1; Matthew 4:1–11). Abram’s vigilance in driving away the birds of prey from the sacrifice corresponds to Christ’s resistance of the devil’s attempts to corrupt his mission. The birds of prey attacking the covenant sacrifice are the type of the forces that oppose every covenant-making act; Christ repels them in the wilderness and ultimately on the cross.
12: As the sun set, a thick and dreadful darkness came over Abram as he fell into a deep sleep — the deep sleep (tardemah) connects to the deep sleep of Adam (Genesis 2:21) in which the rib is taken; in both cases, during the deep sleep a covenant act is accomplished that the sleeping person could not accomplish themselves. The “thick and dreadful darkness” corresponds to the darkness at Sinai (Deuteronomy 4:11) and to the three-hour darkness at the crucifixion (Matthew 27:45). Darkness accompanies divine presence and divine action at moments of covenant-making. The dreadful darkness over Abram is the type of the darkness of judgment that Christ enters and absorbs on the cross so that the covenant of grace can be ratified.
13: “Know for certain that for four hundred years your descendants will be strangers in a country not their own and that they will be enslaved and mistreated” — the Exodus prophecy given to Abraham. Stephen quotes this passage in Acts 7:6–7 in his speech about Israel’s history, reading it as the foundation for understanding Christ as the ultimate deliverer in the line of Moses. The 400 years of slavery that God prophesies are the period after which God will judge the enslaving nation and bring Israel out. Paul in Galatians 3:17 uses the 430-year period (from Exodus 12:40) to argue that the Mosaic law came 430 years after the Abrahamic promise and therefore cannot nullify the prior promise. Christ fulfills the promise that was given 430 years before the law.
14: “But I will punish the nation they serve as slaves, and afterward they will come out with great possessions” — the Exodus promise: Israel will leave Egypt with spoil (fulfilled in Exodus 12:35–36). The divine judgment on the enslaving nation and the liberation with plunder is the type of Christ’s “plundering the strong man’s house” (Matthew 12:29): “how can anyone enter a strong man’s house and carry off his possessions unless he first ties up the strong man? Then he can plunder his house.” Christ binds the strong man (Satan) and plunders his house (liberates those held captive), bringing out his people with the possessions of eternal life. The Exodus pattern of judgment-on-the-enslaver followed by liberation is the template for the cross.
15: “You, however, will go to your ancestors in peace and be buried at a good old age” — the personal promise to Abram of a peaceful death at old age, distinct from the suffering promised to his descendants. The assurance of dying “in peace” is a covenant gift: Simeon at the temple takes the infant Jesus and says, “Sovereign Lord, as you have promised, you may now dismiss your servant in peace. For my eyes have seen your salvation” (Luke 2:29–30). The “in peace” death that God promises Abram finds its fullest form in the death of those who die “in the Lord” (Revelation 14:13: “Blessed are the dead who die in the Lord from now on”). Christ makes it possible for his people to die in the peace that Abram was promised.
16: “In the fourth generation your descendants will come back here, for the sin of the Amorites has not yet reached its full measure” — God delays the Promised Land occupation because the Amorites’ sin has not yet reached the point of judgment. The divine patience with sin before judgment is a recurring NT theme: Paul says the Jews who oppose the gospel “always heap up their sins to the limit” (1 Thessalonians 2:16); Peter says God is “patient… not wanting anyone to perish, but everyone to come to repentance” (2 Peter 3:9). The “full measure of sin” before judgment is the framework for understanding Christ’s coming at the “fullness of time” (Galatians 4:4) — the appointed time when God acts in Christ has its counterpart in the appointed time when God acts in judgment.
17: When the sun had set and darkness had fallen, a smoking firepot with a blazing torch appeared and passed between the pieces — God alone walks through the divided animals, not Abram. This is the theological center of the Abrahamic covenant: God unilaterally ratifies it, making himself alone the covenant-keeper and bearing the covenant curse if the covenant is broken. The unconditional covenant structure — God bearing the oath, not Abram — is the theological foundation for Paul’s argument in Galatians 3:17–18 that human failure cannot nullify the promise. And since God bore the covenant oath, God must bear the covenant curse for breaking it — which is exactly what happens in Christ: “Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us” (Galatians 3:13). God passed between the pieces; God absorbs the judgment.
18: On that day the LORD made a covenant with Abram: “To your descendants I give this land, from the Wadi of Egypt to the great river, the Euphrates” — the covenant (berit) is formally established and the land boundaries are specified. The Abrahamic covenant is the root covenant to which all subsequent OT covenants relate (Mosaic, Davidic, New Covenant). Hebrews 6:13–17 uses the Abrahamic covenant oath to assure believers: “Because God wanted to make the unchanging nature of his purpose very clear to the heirs of what was promised, he confirmed it with an oath… so that, by two unchangeable things in which it is impossible for God to lie, we who have fled to take hold of the hope set before us may be greatly encouraged.” Christ is the mediator (Hebrews 9:15) of the new covenant that fulfills the Abrahamic covenant’s promise.
19: “The land of the Kenites, Kenizzites, Kadmonites” — the first three of the peoples whose land God promises to Abraham’s descendants. The list of ten peoples (vv19–21) is the specific inheritance specification of the covenant. This verse is canonical-context background: the peoples whose land is promised become the peoples among whom Israel will live and must not accommodate (Deuteronomy 7:1–5). The land promise to Abraham’s seed (Galatians 3:16: seed = Christ) is the starting point for the inheritance that the NT spiritualizes: “Blessed are the meek, for they will inherit the earth” (Matthew 5:5); the “land” the meek inherit is the renewed earth of the new creation (Rev 21–22).
20: “Hittites, Perizzites, Rephaites” — three more peoples of the land-promise. The Hittites and Perizzites are mentioned as Canaanite inhabitants throughout the patriarchal narrative (Genesis 23:10; Genesis 26:34; Genesis 34:30). The Rephaites are a people of unusual stature associated with the giants (Deuteronomy 2:11). Their inclusion in the land-promise is canonical-context background for Israel’s later conquest under Joshua and David’s final military defeats of the remaining giants (2 Samuel 21:15–22). The conquest of all these peoples by the covenant people is a type of Christ’s ultimate victory over all powers (1 Corinthians 15:25–27: “he must reign until he has put all his enemies under his feet”).
21: “Amorites, Canaanites, Girgashites and Jebusites” — the final four of the ten peoples whose land is promised. The Jebusites hold Jerusalem until David captures it (2 Samuel 5:6–9), the city that becomes the center of the Davidic kingdom and the site of Christ’s death and resurrection. The land of the ten peoples, promised here to Abraham’s seed, finds its ultimate fulfillment not in the partial conquest of Joshua (Judges 1:27–36: Israel fails to drive out all these peoples) but in Christ, through whom “the meek… will inherit the earth” (Matthew 5:5) and in whom the new creation of the promised land is finally and fully realized (Revelation 21:1: “a new heaven and a new earth”).
